lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: So smile the heavens upon this holy act,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: That after hours with sorrow chide us not!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Amen, amen! but come what sorrow can,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It cannot countervail the exchange of joy

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That one short minute gives me in her sight:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Do thou but close our hands with holy words,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Then love-devouring death do what he dare;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It is enough I may but call her mine.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: These violent delights have violent ends

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And in their triumph die, like fire and powder,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Which as they kiss consume: the sweetest honey

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Is loathsome in his own deliciousness

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And in the taste confounds the appetite:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Therefore love moderately; long love doth so;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Too swift arrives as tardy as too slow.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Good even to my ghostly confessor.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Romeo shall thank thee, daughter, for us both.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: As much to him, else is his thanks too much.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Ah, Juliet, if the measure of thy joy

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Be heap’d like mine and that thy skill be more

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To blazon it, then sweeten with thy breath

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This neighbour air, and let rich music’s tongue

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Unfold the imagined happiness that both

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Receive in either by this dear encounter.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Conceit, more rich in matter than in words,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Brags of his substance, not of ornament:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: They are but beggars that can count their worth;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But my true love is grown to such excess

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I cannot sum up sum of half my wealth.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Come, come with me, and we will make short work;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For, by your leaves, you shall not stay alone

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Till holy church incorporate two in one.

stage_directions.text: Here comes the lady: O, so light a foot Will ne’er wear out the everlasting flint: A lover may bestride the gossamer That idles in the wanton summer air, And yet not fall; so light is vanity.

glosses.definition: the Friar's dire proverb — excessive joy collapses into catastrophe
unite two bodies in one spirit — the sacramental formula

annotations.title: 'These violent delights have violent ends'
annotations.body: At the moment of the wedding — when the Friar is about to perform the sacrament that will, in his plan, end the feud — he speaks this warning. The metaphor is combustive: 'Like fire and powder, / Which, as they kiss, consume.' To call the lovers' union 'fire and powder' is to name the tragedy's engine. The line echoes the Prologue's 'fatal loins' and 'death-mark'd love' and will be fulfilled in 5.3. That the Friar speaks it, knowing what he knows, and proceeds anyway, locates the ethical weight of the Friar's role: he is not naive about the danger, which makes his active help in the secret marriage morally weightier, not less.

trials.prompt: What image does the Friar use for the lovers' union?
options: Fire and gunpowder — immediate detonation on contact

trials.prompt: The Friar sees the danger yet proceeds. What does that show us about his moral position?
options: He is knowingly complicit in a risky plan — which gives his later actions real ethical weight, not the excuse of innocence
He does not believe his own warning
He is only trying to stall
